title: Developer Restructuring Loses Momentum
gist: China's five-year-old property bust is far from resolved: only 17 of 47 defaulted developers have completed debt restructuring, and after 10 deals closed in 2025, just three have finished in the first half of 2026 as Beijing's already-weak support for private developers fades further.

insights.claim: China's property bust is now five years old, having begun in mid-2021 when China Evergrande Group's financial problems started to snowball and hit market confidence, eventually pushing many private-sector developers into formal default.
insights.speaker: Xiaoxi Zhang
insights.so_what: Five years on, the crisis remains unresolved rather than a short-lived shock, implying persistent overhang risk for China property credit and related sectors.
insights.quote: it began in mid-2021 when the financial problems of China Evergrande Group started to snowball, delivering a blow to market confidence and eventually leading many private-sector developers into formal default
insights.locator: China's property bust is now a full five years old

insights.claim: Of the 47 Chinese developers that have defaulted on publicly traded bonds since the crisis began, only 17 have successfully completed an onshore or offshore debt restructuring, leaving the large majority still unresolved.
insights.speaker: Xiaoxi Zhang
insights.so_what: A roughly 36% restructuring completion rate after five years signals bondholders should expect prolonged workout timelines and low recovery certainty on remaining defaulted China property paper.
insights.quote: Out of the 47 developers that have defaulted on publicly traded bonds, only 17 have successfully completed either an onshore or offshore debt restructuring.
insights.locator: Out of the 47 developers that have defaulted

insights.claim: Restructuring activity peaked in 2025 with 10 developers finalizing deals, including large names like Country Garden and Shimao, but has slowed sharply in 2026, with only three completions (Powerlong Real Estate, Fantasia Holdings, and China SCE Group) through the first half of the year.
insights.speaker: Xiaoxi Zhang
insights.so_what: The deceleration from 10 deals in all of 2025 to just 3 in H1 2026 suggests the restructuring pipeline is drying up faster than headline totals imply, a leading indicator for continued distress in remaining defaulted names.
insights.quote: with the year half over, only three developers have completed restructuring agreements: Powerlong Real Estate, Fantasia Holdings, and China SCE Group
insights.locator: Progress seemed to be accelerating in 2025

insights.claim: The Chinese government's support for private-sector developers, which was never very strong to begin with, has weakened further in 2026, and officials appear to have concluded there is little remaining upside in helping these developers restructure.
insights.speaker: Xiaoxi Zhang
insights.so_what: If policymakers are stepping back because they see no further upside, investors should not expect a policy-driven rescue for remaining defaulted private developers, favoring continued caution on China property high-yield credit.
insights.quote: The government’s support for private-sector developers, never very strong, has weakened even further this year.
insights.locator: The government's support for private-sector developers
